key_insight: MoS$_2$ in a biphenylene network shows strongly direction-dependent quantum transport, with the armchair axis acting as a high-current channel and the zigzag axis exhibiting intrinsic negative differential conductance.
engineering_relevance: This anisotropy suggests the same 2D platform could be used as a directional current switch or NDC-based nanoelectronic device, with tunable behavior by device orientation.
method: NEGF quantum transport simulations for I-V response
Thermal and thermoelectric analysis including phonon conductance and zT
evidence_signal: The abstract reports room-temperature phonon thermal conductance of 0.28 nW/K (armchair) and 0.23 nW/K (zigzag), zT peaks of 0.27 and 0.19, and an armchair/zigzag current ratio of about 7×10^4.
watch_item: The results are theoretical and depend on idealized pristine structures, so fabrication defects, contacts, and thermal/environmental effects may reduce the predicted anisotropy and NDC behavior.
paper_title: Direction-Dependent Quantum Transport Properties of MoS$_2$ Integrated into Biphenylene Configuration
